title: Chapter 34: The Spring
content: One year.

Three hundred and sixty-five days since I stood in a white space between worlds and chose this one. Since I let the Fork dissolve around me like sugar in hot water and decided that one life — this life, with this man, in this small Irish town where the rain comes sideways and the cats have opinions about everything — was enough. More than enough.

Clonakilty in spring is a ridiculous thing. The kind of beauty that doesn't ask permission. The harbor turns glassy on still mornings, reflecting cottages in colors that shouldn't work together but somehow do — pink and blue and yellow, like a child's painting that turns out to be a masterpiece. The hills go so green they hurt. Hedge rows burst into white and the air smells like wet earth and something sweet, something nameless, something that makes you breathe deeper without meaning to.
And the café. God, the café.

Julian has been quiet about it. The way he's quiet about everything that matters — not secretive, just... careful. Deliberate. He doesn't announce changes; he just makes them, the way the spring makes changes, and one morning you wake up and the world is different and you didn't even see it happening.

The new menu came first. Not all at once — a soup here, a tart there, a soda bread that made Fiona from the post office cry actual tears and asked if he'd sold his soul. He'd taught himself to cook properly, beyond emergency scones and toast. Real cooking. The kind where you stand in the kitchen at six in the morning with flour on your jaw and a spoon in your hand and you taste and adjust and taste again, patient as a saint, until the thing in the pot becomes what you imagined. I'd watch him sometimes from the doorway, the way he'd close his eyes when he got the seasoning right, like he was hearing music only he could play.

The garden came next. The patch of dirt behind the café that had been a graveyard of broken chairs and empty crates turned into something alive under his hands. Raised beds. A herb spiral. Catmint for the rescues, because of course he planted things for the cats too. Lavender along the fence so the whole garden hums with bees by afternoon.
And then, last month, the sign.
I came in one morning and there it was, hung fresh above the door where the old one had been. Not just "Paws & Pause — Coffee & Cats" anymore. Now it read:
**Paws & Pause — Coffee, Cats & Community.**

He added the "& Community" himself. I didn't ask him to. I didn't even suggest it. He just... did it. Because that's what the café had become — not just a place for coffee and cats, though it was still very much that, but a place where people gathered. Where O'Connell brought his accordion on Fridays and everyone pretended not to love it. Where the knitting circle met on Wednesdays and gossiped in a way that was technically prayers. Where teenagers came after school to do homework near the radiator because it was warmer than home.
& Community. Two words and an ampersand, and they undid me completely.
I have started something too.

It happened by accident, the way the best things do. Máire from the wool shop asked me — once, casually, over a flat white — if I knew how to make a website. I said yes. She said hers hadn't been updated since 2019 and she couldn't afford to pay anyone. I said I'd do it. She offered to pay in yarn. I said deal.

Then Sean from the pottery place asked. Then the woman who makes the seaglass jewelry. Then the teenager — Aisling, sixteen, sharp as a blade, who came into the café one Saturday and said she wanted to learn to code because everyone said she'd have to leave West Cork to have a career and she didn't want to leave but she also didn't want to be stuck.

I started on the first Saturday in March. A free workshop. Digital skills for anyone who showed up. Small business websites, social media for craftspeople, basic coding — Python, HTML, the building blocks. I set up six laptops in the corner of the café (Julian moved three tables and said nothing, just brought tea) and I taught.

Aisling came. So did Máire. So did a farmer named Declan who wanted to sell his cheese online and looked at a computer the way most people look at a loaded gun. So did a pair of thirteen-year-old twins who learned faster than everyone else combined and finished the session by building a website for their dog, which was objectively the best use of anyone's time ever.
It's not Wall Street. It's not Algorithmica. It's not a corner office with a view of the Hudson and a salary with too many zeros and a life I was supposed to want.
It's better.
It's what I actually know, used for people who actually need it.
And on this particular afternoon — golden, the kind of golden that West Cork invents and hoards like a secret — I am in the garden, pruning roses.

Roses. I keep roses alive now. Me. The woman who killed a cactus in college — a cactus, the houseplant equivalent of a cockroach, the one thing that survives neglect, and I managed to dehydrate it in a dorm room so humid the walls sweated. That woman now stands in Irish soil with pruning shears and callused fingers and a knowledge of root systems and deadheading that would make a botanist nod approvingly.
If that's not proof of growth, nothing is.

The thorns catch me — they always catch me, no matter how careful I am, because roses are beautiful and exacting and they draw blood, which is honestly the most honest metaphor for love I've ever encountered. A tiny bead of red wells on my thumb. I wipe it on my jeans and keep working, snipping the dead heads, making room for new bloom.

The garden smells like warm soil and lavender and something green and alive that has no name but fills my chest every time I breathe. The cats are in the window — Biscuit and Marmalade and the new one, Porridge, a tabby with one ear who showed up in January and refused to leave. (Julian said, "Another rescue," in the exact tone of a man who has accepted his fate and is privately thrilled about it.)

I hear the back door before I see him. The creak of the frame, the pad of his steps on the flagstones. He's carrying two mugs of tea — Barry's, always Barry's, because we have standards — and there's a smear of flour on his temple that he hasn't noticed yet.
"There you are," he says, like I might be anywhere else.
"Where else would I be?"

He hands me a mug. The ceramic is warm against my palm, the tea inside dark and strong and exactly the right color, because after a year he knows how I take it down to the shade. He sits beside me on the garden bench — the one he built from reclaimed wood, because of course he builds furniture now — and stretches his legs out. His shoulder touches mine.

The sun is doing something unspeakable to the garden. Turning everything amber and soft. The roses glow like they're lit from inside. Somewhere down the street, someone is playing fiddle music from an open window, and it drifts over the rooftops like a spell.
"You know what's funny?" he says.

I look at him. His face is golden in this light. The lines around his eyes — from laughing, from squinting at recipes, from being alive and thirty and here — are deeper than they were a year ago. I want to kiss every one of them.

"I spent three years running from place to place," he says. "Bangkok to Berlin to Buenos Aires. Always a new city, always a new cafe to work in, always a new face to learn and forget. And now—" He looks at the garden. At the herbs and the lavender and the cats in the window and the bench he built and the café he's still transforming, slowly, quietly, the way he transforms everything. "Now I can't imagine leaving this one."

I lean into him. His arm comes around me, automatic, like breathing. My head finds the hollow of his shoulder, the spot that was made for me, or maybe the spot that became mine through sheer persistence.
"Choice paralysis, meet the cure," I say.
He laughs — that low sound that lives in my chest like a second heartbeat. "Is that what I am? The cure for choice paralysis?"
"No," I say. "You're the reason I stopped being paralyzed. The cure was choosing."
He's quiet for a moment. Then his arm tightens around me, and he presses his lips to the top of my head, and I feel the shape of his smile against my hair.

The tea cools in my hands. I don't care. I'd let a hundred mugs go cold for this — for the weight of him beside me, for the rose-scratched earth under my boots, for the sound of the fiddle winding through the Clonakilty air like it's tracing the shape of a life.
He shifts. Takes my left hand.

I look down at it — at the small scars from café work, the thin white lines where the steam wand got me twice and the knife slipped once. At the calluses from gardening, rough patches on my palm and the pads of my fingers. At the lines that come from being thirty and alive, from gripping and reaching and holding on.
He studies my hand like it's a map he's memorizing. Turns it over, slowly, traces the lines with his thumb. Then he places something in my palm.
A ring.

Not a diamond. A Claddagh. The traditional Irish ring — two hands holding a heart, and above the heart, a crown. The metal is warm from his pocket, or maybe from his hand, or maybe from something else entirely. It has weight. Not heavy — present. Real.
"My grandmother's," he says. "Margaret gave it to me when she visited. Said it was time."

His voice is steady. His hands are not. I can feel the fine tremor in his fingers against mine, and it undoes me, it hollows me out and fills me back up, because this man — this man who runs into burning buildings without flinching, who restarts his life in a new country like it's nothing, who added "& Community" to a sign because he meant it — this man is shaking while he holds a ring out to me, and that's the bravest thing I've ever seen.
"Julian Gallagher," I say. I'm looking at the ring. Then at him. Then at the ring again, because I can't quite believe it's real. "Are you asking me something?"
"I'm asking you everything."
God. Oh, god.

I look at the Claddagh in my palm. The hands for friendship. The heart for love. The crown for loyalty. Three symbols, intertwined, inseparable. You can't have one without the others. They hold each other the way he holds me — like the whole thing falls apart if any single piece lets go.

And I think about the Garden of Forking Paths. About an impossible place where every choice birthed a new world and every world was real and none of them were the one I wanted. About the Composite Julian — the one made of every possibility, every could-have-been, every path not taken — who looked at me and said: Choose the one where you're most yourself.

I think about all the paths I walked. The ones where I stayed in New York and became someone's footnote. The ones where I chose Algorithmica and elegant loneliness. The ones where I chose other men, other cities, other versions of alright. And this path — this one path, through a café with too many cats and a town that doesn't close at midnight, through flour on his jaw and soil under my nails and a life so specific, so improbable, so deeply mine that no other version of me could have lived it.
I slide the ring on.

Not my right hand. The Irish tradition for friendship, for courtship, for the in-between spaces of almost and not-yet. Not my right hand, where right-side-out means single and heart-facing-out means taken-ish but still deciding.
My left hand. Heart pointing inward. Toward me, toward my pulse, toward the center of everything I am.
That means claimed. Chosen. Forever.

Julian stares at my hand. At the ring sitting on my finger — his grandmother's ring, Margaret's ring, a ring that's been worn by women who chose and were chosen in turn, a ring that carries the weight of a family's yes. He stares at it like he can't believe it's real, like he's the one who's been given something impossible, like he's the one who just glitched into a universe better than the one he expected.
"Is that a yes?" he whispers.
And I kiss him.

In the garden. In the spring. In the only timeline I have — the only one I need, the only one I've ever truly wanted. Roses in the dirt and cats in the window and the smell of coffee drifting from the café, that deep dark roast that means home. The fiddle still playing from down the street, like Clonakilty itself is celebrating. The lavender and the bees and the golden light that makes everything look like it's been dipped in honey. The soil under my boots and the warmth of his mouth and the prickle where my rose-thorn thumb touches his jaw.

He kisses me back. His hand — the one that was shaking — finds my face, cups my cheek like I'm something precious and breakable and worth holding carefully. My hands find his collar, his shoulders, the familiar geography of him that I've mapped so many times it's more home than any address I've ever had.
"It's a yes," I whisper against his lips. "It's always been a yes. In every world."

He pulls back just far enough to look at me. His eyes are wet. Not crying — the way they get when something is so true it can't be contained, when the feeling is bigger than the body and has to escape somehow. I know, because mine are doing the same thing.
"In every world," he repeats. Like he's tasting it. Like he's memorizing it. Like it's a promise he intends to keep across every version of reality that ever was or will be.

The Claddagh sits on my left hand. Two hands, a heart, a crown. Path, choice, and love. The same symbols the Fork app used to sort my lives, to weight my options, to calculate the mathematics of who I might become. But now they're metal instead of code. Real instead of digital. Chosen instead of forced.
I chose this. Not because an algorithm told me to. Not because the math worked out. Not because it was safe or smart or the highest-scoring option on some cosmic spreadsheet.
I chose it because it's where I'm most myself. Because the woman who kills cacti is dead, and the woman who keeps roses alive is here, and she's never going back.

Julian kisses me again. The cats watch from the window with the profound disinterest of creatures who have never once doubted they were the center of the universe. The tea goes cold. The golden hour deepens into dusk. And on my left hand, heart pointed inward, a ring shines — worn and warm and real — like an answer to a question I finally know how to ask.
*What do you choose?*
*This. Always this. In every world, in every timeline, in every version of the story — this.*
